content:
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ego_2">: not
							emphatic itself, but expressed merely to set off </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vos_2">,
							which is. The Latin is so fond of putting pronouns in contrast that one
							is often (as here) expressed for the mere purpose of antithesis. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="judices_2">:
							not <gloss>judges</gloss>, but rather <gloss>jurors</gloss>. They were
							persons selected by law to try facts (under the presidency of a <foreign xml:lang="lat">praetor</foreign> or <foreign xml:lang="lat">judex
							quaestionis</foreign>), and varied in number from a single one to fifty
							or more. They were originally selected from the Senators, but C.
							Gracchus had transferred the right to sit as <foreign xml:lang="lat">
							judices</foreign> to the <foreign xml:lang="lat">equites</foreign> or
							wealthy middle class). Sulla, whose reforms went into operation <date when="-0080">B.C. 80</date>, had restored this right to the
							Senators, and the present case was the first to occur under the new
							system. It was brought in the <foreign xml:lang="lat">Quaestio
								inter sicarios</foreign> (or court for the trial of murder), under
							the presidency of the praetor M. Fannius. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quid_quod_2">
							. <gloss>Why it is that</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_2">
							(causal) . . . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="surrexerim_2">
							expresses <emph>a fact</emph>, and takes the subj. of informal ind.
							disc. as depending on the indirect question </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quid_sit_2">.
							§ <ref n="AG 592">592</ref>, 1 (341, b); cf. B. 323; G. 663.1; H. 652
							(529,ii) H-B. 535, I, a. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="summi_nobilissimi_2">:
							notice the chiastic order: § <ref n="AG 598">598</ref>, f (344, f); B.
							350, II, c; G. 682; H. 666, 2 (562) H-B 628 </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_sedeant_2">
							: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_2"> has
							a slight concessive force: render by <gloss> when</gloss> or <gloss>
							while</gloss>; <gloss>though</gloss> would be too strong. Since Sulla's
							victory had restored the aristocracy to power, it might be expected that
							men of rank (<foreign xml:lang="lat">nobilissimi</foreign>) would have
							courage to come forward and defend Roscius: their presence showed their
							sympathies, though they did not rise to defend him. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ego_2_2">:
							emphatic, as opposed to the orators and men of rank. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="potissimum_2">
							, <gloss>rather than any other</gloss>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aetate_2">:
							Cicero was but twenty-six years old. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sim_2">: in
							direct disc. this might be either subj. to indicate the character of
							Cicero, or indic. to denote a mere fact about him; here it is
							necessarily subj. as being an integral part of the clause </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_2_2"> . .
							. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="surrexerim_2_2">,
							which is itself dependent on </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quid_sit_2_2">;
							§ <ref n="AG 593">593</ref> (342); B. 324,1; (G. 663,1; H. 652, I
							(529, ii); H.-B. 539. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sedeant_2">, <gloss>sit
							still</gloss>, instead of rising to speak: subj. of integral part,
							dependent on </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sim_comparandus_2">
							.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hi_2">:
							strongly demonstrative; accompanied, perhaps, with a gesture, <gloss>these
							men here</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="injuriam_2">
							,<gloss>injustice</gloss> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="novo_scelere_2">
							(abl. of means), <gloss>the strange</gloss> (almost = unheard of) <gloss>
							charge</gloss> (of parricide). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="oportere_2">:
							this verb is always impersonal ; its subject here is the clause </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="injuriam_defendi_2">
							. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="defendi_2">,</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="defendere_2">:
							see Vocab.; supply <gloss>but</gloss> (suggested in Latin by the close
							juxtaposition of the two infs.) before </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="defendere_2_2">
							in translating. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="iniquitatem_temporum_2">,
							i.e. the disturbed state of politics, while the wounds of the Civil War
							were still fresh. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ita_fit_2">:
							the subject is the clause </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_adsint_2">,
							etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="adsint_2">,<gloss>they
							attend</gloss>: opposed to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="taceant_2">;
							the position of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="taceant_2_2">
							indicates this antithesis. The friends of any party to a suit attended
							court to give him the advantage of their presence and influence (cf.
							Caes. B.G. 1. 4). Such friends were technically called <foreign xml:lang="lat">advocati</foreign>, but they did not, like the modern <gloss>
							advocate</gloss>, speak in court. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="officium_2">
							,<gloss>duty</gloss>, arising from their relations to the murdered man,
							who had stood in the relation of <gloss>hospitality</gloss> (see <gloss>
							hospes</gloss> in Vocab.) with some of the highest families. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="audacissimus_2">,
							i.e. is it that I have more effrontery than any of the rest? </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ne_quidem_2">
							,<gloss>not . . . either</gloss>, endorsing, as usual, the emphatic
							word: § <ref n="AG 322">322</ref>, f (151, e); B. 151, e; G. 448, N.2;
							H. (569, iii, 2). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="istius_2">,
							i.e. that which is in your thoughts: § <ref n="AG 297">297</ref>, c
							(102, c); B. 87; G. 306; H.507, 3 (450); H.-B. 271, a. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sim_2_2">,<foreign xml:lang="lat">conjunctivus modestiae</foreign>: §<ref n="AG 447">
							447</ref>, vi (311, b); Cf. B. 280, 2 ; G. 257,1; H. 556 (486, i);
							H.-B. 519,1, b. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aliis_2">,
							dat.: § <ref n="AG 381"> 381</ref> (229); B. 180, 2, d; G. 345; H.
							429, 2 (386, 2); H.-B. 371. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="praereptam_2">:
							prae- gives here the force of <gloss>getting the start of
								others</gloss> in snatching it (cf. <gloss> prevent</gloss>, from <foreign xml:lang="lat">praevenio</foreign>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="me_2">: so
							emphatic as to throw </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="igitur_2">
							out of its usual place. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="reciperem_2">
							,<gloss>undertake</gloss> a case offered; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="suscipere_2">
							is to take up of one's own motion. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="amplitudo_2">
							,<gloss>position</gloss>, from birth, wealth, office, or the like. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="id_quod_2">,<gloss>a
							thing which</gloss>: §<ref n="AG 307">307</ref>, d (200, e); G. 614,
							R.2; H.399,6 (445, 7); H.-B. 325, a and N.2. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dixisset_2">,
							an integral part of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="putaretur_2">
							. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="putaretur_2_2">:
							apodosis of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fecisset_2">;
							§<ref n="AG 517">517</ref> (308); B. 304, I; G. 597; H. 579 (510);
							H.-B. 581. The whole from </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_verbum_2">
							through </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="putaret_2">
							is the apodosis of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_dixisset_2">
							in 1.14. Translate, <gloss>if any one had spoken, in case he had
								made any allusion to politics, he would</gloss>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ego_3">,
							etc., <gloss>but in</gloss> MY <gloss>case, even if I</gloss>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="etiamsi_dixero_3">,
							. . .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="poterit_3">:
							§<ref n="AG 516">516</ref>, c (307, c); B. 264, a; G. 244, 2; H. 574,
							2 (508, 2); cf. H.-B. 494 and 579, a. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="similiter_3">
							,<gloss>in like manner</gloss>, i.e. as if a man of rank had spoken. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="exire_3">,
							etc., i.e. this speech will not be quoted and talked over, and hence any
							allusions to politics which it may contain will not seem more
							significant than they really are. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="emanare_3">
							not to be confounded with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="manere_3">. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="deinde_3">
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_3">:
							the second reason, corresponding to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quia_3"> in
							1. 14. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ceterorum_3">,
							opposed to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ego_3_2"> in
							1.6, below. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dictum_3">:
							noun, limited by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ceterorum_3_2">
							;</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dicto_3">
							(l.5) is also a noun, though modified by an adv. ; § <ref n="AG 321">
							321</ref>, b (207, c) ; G. 437, R. ; H.-B. 250, 2, b, N. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="concedi_3">,
							impersonal: § <ref n="AG 372">372</ref> (230); B. 187, ii, b; G. 217;
							H.426, 3 (384, 5); H.-B. 364, 2. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nondum_accessi_3">,
							I have not yet gone into public life, i.e. become candidate for any
							office. Cicero began his political career five years later, with the
							quaestorship. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tametsi_3">,<gloss>
							although</gloss>, in its so-called "corrective" use, — the concession
							coming after the general statement, as a kind of limitation of it. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ignoscendi_ratio_3">
							,<gloss>the idea of pardon</gloss>. The vaguely general word <foreign xml:lang="lat">ratio</foreign> with the gen. of the gerund expresses
							little more than our word <gloss> pardoning</gloss> alone. The Latin,
							being poor in abstract words, has to resort to such shifts as this to
							supply their place. So </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cognoscendi_consuetudo_3">
							,<gloss>the habit of judicial investigation</gloss>, is almost
							equivalent to <gloss>judicial investigation</gloss> simply. This was a
							bold speech to make under the rule of the tyrant Sulla. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="accedit_4">,<gloss>there
							is in addition</gloss>: used as a kind of passive of <foreign xml:lang="lat">addo</foreign> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illa_4">,<gloss>
							this</gloss>, i.e. the following (a common use of this pronoun). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_4">,<gloss>
							that</gloss>: §<ref n="AG 572">572</ref>(333); B. 299,1,b; G. 525, 1;
							H.588,3 (540, iv); H.-B. 549,550, 552, 1. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="a_ceteris_4">
							,<gloss>from</gloss>
								<gloss>the others</gloss>, i.e. the nobles. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="petitum_sit_4">:
							for subjunctive see § <ref n="AG 447">447</ref>, a and N. (334,g and
							N.) ; G. 457, 2, N.; H. (p.267, footnote 1); H.-B. 517,1. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_dicerent_4">
							[<foreign xml:lang="lat"> causam</foreign>], subst. clause of purpose,
							subj. of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="petitum_sit_4_2">:
							§<ref n="AG 566">566</ref> (331, h) ; G. 546; H.565, 2 (499, 3); cf.
							H.-B. 502, 3, a. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dicere_causam_4">
							is the technical expression for defending a case. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_arbitrarentur_4">:
							a clause of result, dependent on </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ita_sit_4">:
							§<ref n="AG 537">537</ref> and N.2 (319 and a.) ; B. 284, 1; G. 552;
							H. 591 (500 and N.1); H.-B. 521, 2 and a. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="utrumvis_4">
							,<gloss>either</gloss> [course, i.e. to speak or be silent], <gloss>at
							their choice</gloss> ; lit. <gloss>either</gloss> [of the two] <gloss>you
							please</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="salvo_officio_4">
							(abl. of manner), <gloss>without a breach of
								duty</gloss>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="arbitrarentur_4">:
							imperf. following </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="petitum_sit_4_3">,
							which is regarded as a secondary tense since it represents the perf.
							indic. ; § <ref n="AG 485">485</ref>, a (287, a) ; B. 268, I; G. 511,
							N.2; H.546 (495, i); H.-B. 481. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="a_autem_4">,
							etc., lit. <gloss>but from <emph>me</emph>
								</gloss>,
							etc. (opposed to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="a_ceteris_4_2">
							above). The emphasis may be preserved by changing the construction in
							English: <gloss>but as for myself, men have urged
								it</gloss> [i.e. that I should undertake the defence of Roscius] <gloss>on
							me who</gloss>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ei_4">,<gloss>
							men</gloss> ; here used simply as a correlative to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qui_4">, and
							not in a really demonstrative sense. The reference is of course to the
							noble friends of Roscius </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="debeam_4">,
							subj. of characteristic: § <ref n="AG 535">535</ref>(320); B. 283,1;
							G. 631, 2; H. 591, I (503, i); H.-B. 521, 1. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="his_4">,
							emphatic, summing up the reasons he has given for undertaking the case, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ego_4">
							(next line), emphatic as opposed to the others present. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="patronus_4">
							,<gloss>advocate</gloss>, the word <foreign xml:lang="lat">advocati</foreign>
							having a different meaning (see note on <ref n="pl2.7">p. 2, l. 7</ref>,
							above). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="unus_4">,<gloss>as
							the one man</gloss> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="uti_ne_4">:
							in purpose clauses the double form is often used instead of <foreign xml:lang="lat">ne</foreign> alone. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="desertus_4">,
							etc.: observe that Cicero not only attempts to win the sympathies of the
							jurors for the helpIessness of his client, but that he also contrives to
							suggest, in advance of the formal statement of facts, that there is a
							combination or conspiracy of some kind against young Roscius The same
							thing was insinuated in sect. vi by the use of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="_conflatam_4">
							(1.6). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hujusce_5">,<gloss>of
							my</gloss> client. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="municeps_Amerinus_5">
							,<gloss>a citizen of the free town Ameria</gloss>. The Latin uses an
							adj. of possession when it can, often where the English prefers <gloss>
							of</gloss>: §<ref n="AG 343">343</ref>, a (190); B. 354, 4; G. 362,
							R.1; H. (395, N.2). Cf. <ref n="Verg. A. 2.55">Aeneid, 2.
								55</ref>,<ref n="Verg. A. 2.487">487</ref>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hospitiis_5">
							,<gloss> guest-friendships</gloss>. The <foreign xml:lang="lat">
							hospitium</foreign> was a relation between individuals of different
							cities or states, at a time when there were no international relations ;
							it included the duties of hospitality and protection, was transmitted
							from father to son, and was vouched for by a ticket (<foreign xml:lang="lat">tessera</foreign>). Roscius not only had this formal
							relation to several of the greatest families at Rome, but he was also on
							intimate terms of personal friendship with them. Hence, in line 27, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="domesticus_consuetudo_5">
							,<gloss>intercourse and companionship</gloss> [with them] <gloss>in
							their homes</gloss>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="honestatis_gratia_5">
							(so </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="honoris_causa_5">,
							§<ref n="AG 17">17</ref> ),<gloss>with all honor</gloss>. It seems to
							have been held a liberty to mention the name of any person of quality in
							a public address ; hence such mention is generally accompanied by a form
							of compliment. Cf. the modern parliamentary usage of referring to
							members of a deliberative body by the names of their offices (or as the

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quo_modo_31">
							,<gloss>how</gloss>. Cicero contends that he is not obliged to discuss
							the manner of the murder, since Erucius has not raised that point, and
							has not even been able to assign a motive. His own position in the
							argument is so strong, however, that, he says, he can afford to concede
							a point by waiving the question of <gloss>motive</gloss> and allowing
							Erucius to argue the case on the basis of the <gloss>means</gloss> by
							which Roscius could have committed the crime. This is of course a
							rhetorical device to introduce one of Cicero's strongest arguments. It
							cannot be proved that it was even possible for Roscius to kill his
							father under the circumstances. By calling for the details of the murder
							Cicero shows that none can be produced. The whole passage serves also as
							an effective preparation for the countercharge (omitted in this
							edition), in which it is shown that Sex. Roscius Magnus had not only a
							motive, but every opportunity. </gloss>
